raw_output_payload.reasoning: We need a bash command to produce a table merging lines where first field matches. Likely use join. Need to keep header line starting with Gene. So cat files, sort, join. Command: join -t ' ' -1 1 -2 1 <(sort file1) <(sort file2) > output. But keep Gene line from file1? Use cat file1 | grep '^Gene' > header; then join rest. So: { grep '^Gene' /testbed/dir1/subdir1/textfile4.txt; join -t ' ' -1 1 -2 1 <(grep -v '^Gene' /testbed/dir1/subdir1/textfile4.txt | sort) <(grep -v '^Gene' /testbed/dir2/subdir2/textfile5.txt | sort); } > merged.txt. Provide single command.
raw_output_payload.reasoning_content: We need a bash command to produce a table merging lines where first field matches. Likely use join. Need to keep header line starting with Gene. So cat files, sort, join. Command: join -t ' ' -1 1 -2 1 <(sort file1) <(sort file2) > output. But keep Gene line from file1? Use cat file1 | grep '^Gene' > header; then join rest. So: { grep '^Gene' /testbed/dir1/subdir1/textfile4.txt; join -t ' ' -1 1 -2 1 <(grep -v '^Gene' /testbed/dir1/subdir1/textfile4.txt | sort) <(grep -v '^Gene' /testbed/dir2/subdir2/textfile5.txt | sort); } > merged.txt. Provide single command.

task.query: Create a table containing all information from /testbed/dir1/subdir1/textfile4.txt and /testbed/dir2/subdir2/textfile5.txt, merging lines where the first field of both files matches, and keeping the line that starts with "Gene" at the start of the file.
task.gold: join -a1 -a2 <(sed s/^Gene/00ne/ /testbed/dir1/subdir1/textfile4.txt | sort) <(sed s/^Gene/00ne/ /testbed/dir2/subdir2/textfile5.txt | sort) | column -t | sed s/^00ne/Gene/
